title: Resizing and expected complexity

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Resizing and expected complexity; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Resizing and expected complexity
sources.claim: Hash tables resize and rehash when load policy threatens probe or chain performance, producing amortized rather than worst-case constant insertion.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Hash tables resize and rehash when load policy threatens probe or chain performance, producing amortized rather than worst-case constant insertion.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Doubling capacity requires recomputing each bucket because bucket index depends on the new capacity. Your goal is to implement or simulate rehashing and derive its amortized effect. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Array representation and operations; Linked lists; Collision resolution; Time, space and asymptotic growth. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Hash tables resize and rehash when load policy threatens probe or chain performance, producing amortized rather than worst-case constant insertion. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, implement or select a table with explicit hash, equality, capacity, and ownership; in C++, unordered containers require compatible hash/equality and stable keys; in Java, HashMap/HashSet depend on equals and hashCode; in Python, dict/set require hashable keys with consistent __eq__ and __hash__.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Capacity choice, maximum load, incremental rehash, and allocation failure vary by implementation. The draft therefore rejects: Existing bucket indexes remain valid after capacity changes.

includes: Relate capacity and load factor to amortized expected operations and adversarial caveats.
Implement or simulate rehashing and derive its amortized effect.

In C, implement or select a table with explicit hash, equality, capacity, and ownership; in C++, unordered containers require compatible hash/equality and stable keys; in Java, HashMap/HashSet depend on equals and hashCode; in Python, dict/set require hashable keys with consistent __eq__ and __hash__.
excludes: Capacity choice, maximum load, incremental rehash, and allocation failure vary by implementation.

outcomes: Implement or simulate rehashing and derive its amortized effect.

examples.title: Resizing and expected complexity: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: Doubling capacity requires recomputing each bucket because bucket index depends on the new capacity.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: Doubling capacity requires recomputing each bucket because bucket index depends on the new capacity.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Rehash stored entries when capacity changes; bucket placement depends on both hash and current capacity.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because Rehash stored entries when capacity changes; bucket placement depends on both hash and current capacity.

examples.title: Resizing and expected complexity: implementation transfer
examples.situation: In C, implement or select a table with explicit hash, equality, capacity, and ownership; in C++, unordered containers require compatible hash/equality and stable keys; in Java, HashMap/HashSet depend on equals and hashCode; in Python, dict/set require hashable keys with consistent __eq__ and __hash__.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, implement or select a table with explicit hash, equality, capacity, and ownership; in C++, unordered containers require compatible hash/equality and stable keys; in Java, HashMap/HashSet depend on equals and hashCode; in Python, dict/set require hashable keys with consistent __eq__ and __hash__.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Capacity choice, maximum load, incremental rehash, and allocation failure vary by implementation.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: Existing bucket indexes remain valid after capacity changes.

analogy.example: A carefully labeled physical arrangement can introduce Resizing and expected complexity, while the canonical invariant and operations remain the actual algorithmic model.
usefulFor: Introducing structure, order, connectivity, or state transition before formal notation
limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.
